titleStmt.title: Letter from William Upton Tyrrell to Elizabeth Tyrrell 10, June, 1917
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: William Upton Tyrrell

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: William Upton Tyrrell is experiencing good weather. He acknowledges receipt of a parcel.

msIdentifier.repository: OPW-Maynooth University Archive & Research Centre at Castletown

correspAction.placeName: Ballindoolin House, Ballindoolin, Edenderry, Kings Co., Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
10 June 1917 My dearest Mamy. Today being Sunday I will try to
write you a decent letter tho' there is
very little to write about. First of all
I am still here and the report that
Young our signalling officer was
wounded is untrue so I am not
wanted yet. Things are absolutely
splendid and everything went like
clockwork. There are a number of little
incidents that I am afraid to
mention that are really very amusing.
I will be able to tell you a little later
on. I see Lloyd G. was called up on the
morning of the 7th to hear mines go
up. Well, that is nothing compared to
the fact that they blew out in South
America at the wrong side of the
earth and caused an earthquake. I went over yesterday afternoon
and had tea with the battalion I
used to be with. It was great to
see so many old friends again. I think Whitfelt and another fellow
are coming over here today if they
can get away. I found a lot there
whom I know. It is a They are
about 6 miles from here and as I
was walking there I saw a fellow
coming along in a motor and
so I stopped him and asked him
where he was going. He was
passing where I wanted to go so
he gave me a lift. He was a staff
officer too so you see there
is nothing like cheek is there. I
came back on a motor lorrie. On one memorable occasion last
year tow officers of this batallion
were going back rather late
having had a very good dinner
and hailed a car passing.
When they got in great was there
surprise to find their own general in
it. He was delighted to give them a
lift. The weather is still very fine and
rather too hot though not so bad
as it was. We have nothing to do here
except wait about. We just do
some work to pass the time. I had a great letter from Minnie
on Friday evening with all the Bray
news. I am writing to her today. I can't think of any news now. By the way I did get all the clothes
you sent. I always forgot to tell
you so in previous letters. Also
please send envelopes. They are
always scarce and you see I use
quite a lot of them. I have lots of
paper. An occasional book is
always welcome but only put in
6d ones as I can't cart them
about so just give them to
some one else when I have
finished with them. I won't write tomorrow unless
I have something to write about
as it is wast of paper. With best love to all Your most affec Son Willie I was delighted to get Daddy's
letter last night late. Of course I
had to give up the company but I was
not temp Capt. I would much
rather have the coy not than the
signalling as things stand. I have
a chance of getting back with a
company when I rejoin as the
sig. off. is alright.
